1/ make all arguments Params
2/ include all arguments in the constructor, and make the constructor @keyword_only (can copy over the code at https://github.com/databricks/spark-deep-learning/blob/53e5f56aefc0b1766c1b3424814357deeca36d7b/python/sparkdl/transformers/param.py#L32... which is copied over from pyspark because it is only available for Spark 2.1.1+ and we wanted compatibility for earlier versions...)
3/ for the Params we want to ensure are specified, put in validation logic accordingly.

This way there is no need to worry about what makes it into the constructor and what doesn't and we can keep everything consistent.

@liuzx32 The way that Scala works is more like playing around shared pointers. When we create a NDArray, we request a pointer that point to a memory space on the C++ side. If we want to release this piece of memory, we need to call dispose somewhere in the Scala code in order to get it executed. However, if we forgot to do that, Scala/JVM will discard this pointer rather than helping us release the memory and that cause the memory leak.

The binary size increases about 0.1% for both shared and static library (CUDA, CUDNN, MKL). Compiling mxnet already takes quite long time, so the relative increase of the compile time is also tiny.

I'm working with some variants of RBM and the use of `.astype('float32')` in several places increases training time over 20%. In the case of usual basic RBM, it increases about 10% of training time in my [test](https://github.com/asitstands/incubator-mxnet/blob/e96c79d84e00e5b7d26b18c8faba0daaac812f5e/example/restricted-boltzmann-machine/binary_rbm.py#L86) for mnist. Of course, it depends on the hyperparameters and data. However, I think that, in general, the cost cannot be ignored for applications using heavy Monte Carlo samplings of discrete states.

As `C["Vc"]` is scalar this could be revised avoiding loops:

```python
    def _get_site_amplification_linear(self, vs30, C):
        """
        Compute site amplification linear term,
        equation (11), pag 1992.
        """
        # Inititalise with the Vs30 > Vc case as the default
        return_array = (C["c"] * np.log(C["Vc"] / C["vref"])) *\
            np.ones_like(vs30)
        # If any sites have Vs30 <= Vc then apply only to that subset
        idx = vs30 < C["Vc"]
        if np.any(idx):
            return_array[idx] = C["c"] * np.log(vs30[idx] / C["vref"])

        return return_array
```

To nitpick a bit, that may be what you get when you call a `zope.browserresource.interfaces.IResource`, but that's not guaranteed by the (default) ++resource namespace. The (default) resource namespace, as implemented in `zope.traversing.namespace.resource`, simply finds the default named adapter for the request. The interface that adapter implements is not specified. *This* object puts a further (previously implicit) constraint upon it that it is callable. This object doesn't even require that the results of calling it be a string.

This object is further derived with objects like JavaScriptViewlet that use templates and assume that getURL returns something that is usable as a string in a TALES context. Perhaps those objects could use more documentation, and **certainly** the common usage and interaction with zope.browserresource could also be called out more clearly.

Multiple layers of that process can be overridden by other frameworks, starting with different/more specific registrations for ++resource. One framework that I work on doesn't use zope.browserresource at all, but does have objects that we use as resources.

(This is neither here nor there, but the reason I've been working on a lot of this documentation lately is that I've been implementing a [ZPT-based template system](http://ntinikola-chameleon.readthedocs.io/en/latest/) for the [nikola static website generator](https://getnikola.com/handbook.html). It's outside of any Zope3 framework, so I had to put a lot of these pieces together again for myself as I wrote and documented it; I'm *still* learning things as I help with this documentation---and your comments have been very helpful.)

Not sure about running one target root at a time being the default. I generally think that the default should support the fastest/healthiest scenario, namely a repo that has globally consistent 3rdparty deps. If your repo has pathologies, then we can support slower modes, but you should be the one to have to modify your config, not everyone else who kept their repos clean... 

Granted, the pathology in this case may be beyond your control, if the `x` deps in your example above are indirect via some different 3rdparty deps, but that's probably an esoteric case.

In other words, I would much rather that `./pants test ::` be fast, and have to do some explaining in the pathological case, than have it be slow and have to explain why to every evaluator of pants.

I think this highlights the difficulty of working with `IGangaFile` given its dual role as both input and output files which is especially compounded by the special case of `LocalFile`.

For a standard `IGangaFile`, `put` should copy the file from the local machine to some remote place and `get` should copy the file from come remote place to the local machine. Normally a job using a `IGangaFile` as an input would read the file *from* the remote location whereas a job using it as an output would write it *to* there when done. When used as an inputfile the schema attributes need to be interpreted differently to when it's an output file: e.g. `MassStorageFile`'s `namePattern` is used to find the file on the remote file system when it's an input file but is used to find the file on the WN system when it's an outputfile.

As well as input/output differences there's also the difference between file handling done on the WN (e.g. `DiracFile`) and files sent back via the sandbox to be handled on the client (e.g. `LocalFile`). There's different codepaths and potentially different meanings to the schema attributes.

Add to this the complications of subfiles and filename globbing the whole system is very unintuitive. We need to be very clear on the API to which we expect `IGangaFile`s to conform and exactly how it should be used before we start implementing it. For example, we already have a way for the user to specify where the file should be copied to in the `LocalFile`/output situation which is `localDir`. Adding another argument to `get()` just confuses this. What should be done instead is make sure that all `IGangaFile` subclasses have a `localDir` schema attribute and make sure that is used consistently; It's always the source directory for a `put()` and the destination directory for a `get()`. Likewise, for `namePattern` (only ever a file glob within `localDir` for inputfile/`put`, within some WN dir for an outputfile and within the remote dir for a `get`).

PTAL - I actually decided to implement this, thanks for the tip!  Now it does a streaming gunzip, and indeed that eliminates the 20 millisecond delay in TTFB when doing on-demand decompression - it's <5 ms to the first byte returned now when decompressing on demand :)

The Content-Length hassles would have been a dealbreaker IMO, but as it so happens gzip files encode the length of the original uncompressed content in their last four bytes (well, strictly speaking they record the lower 32 bits of that length, but I think we can rely on not having pre-compressed blobs larger than 2^32 bytes = 4GB).  So we can extract those and use them to send the Content-Length header before we've decompressed a single byte.

This scheme does appear to be an improvement over the previous. I like how the format and value are brought together on the same line.  This allows us to avoid counting lines to learn which format goes with which value.  

What is lacking in both of these schemes is a formal contract regarding the order of the elements in the buffer.  There does not exist a template which can be used by both sender and receiver of this packet to guarantee that the formats and order used by both will be the same.  For instance, for one to write the host side code that will unpack this message, one must look at the NCP side code, here and manually construct a block of code that will unpack the message.  One cannot change the order of these contents without ensuring that the order is changed on all the hosts that have implemented a solution to the older order/format.

The only way I can see to solve this, would be to move the encoding and decoding of the message out of ncp_... and into spinel itself or perhaps some new source file that could be shared by both the host and NCP.

In general, I believe OpenThread should provide mechanisms but not policy wherever possible.  As @DuaneEllis-TI mentioned, there are a number of approaches to optimizing performance.  Simply stating that the platform should enter "high power" or "performance needed" state whenever the Commissioner/Joiner roles are enabled is a specification of policy.  What if another platform has a more fine-grained approach to optimizing performance of crypto operations?

I would prefer to provide notifications in changes to the Commissioner/Joiner state.  Then users could do what they want with that notification, some of which may not be related to optimizing crypto performance at all.

@pcppcp No, `and` and `or` doesn't necessarily return booleans. Their behavior in python (and most of the programming languages) are like: `and` -> _if left side is truty, return right side, or else return left side_; `or` -> _if left side is truty, return left side, else return right side_. That happen to do the boolean operations when you're dealing with booleans or any truthable value.
This makes a very common pattern to use it to safeguard expressions like above: if left side returns `None` (falsy), returns this `None` (as there's no `_client` instance in the class yet), else returns right side, which is the access of the attribute on `_client`, be it a valid `user_id`, or `None` again.

> This seems semi-arbitrary to me; can you remind me why we need to do this?

For one, because we explicitly do not upload any @pulumi node_modules.   So if we actually capture a pulumi-module *by* reference, then that will def break at runtime. :)

This is both because that node_module is enormous, and because much of it does not work on the 'inside' due to needing to be able to do things like speak to the pulumi engine.

> so it's not clear that by definition eliding them won't lead someone to be in a situation where they can't access important functionality (like a helper function). 

Note: using a helper function is fine.  In essence, that's what is happening when you use "pulumi.Config".  If you actually use anything *in* the module, then that is captured by value 'as expected', and it serialized properly into your lambda code.  

What is being prevented here is literally being able to actually use the module-object itself.  

> If it's just that gRPC is too large, why don't we filter out native modules, 

I'm not sure what counts as a native module :)   We definitely don't want to prevent a module that *uses* anything native.  in practice, most modules will end up doing that in some manner.  

> or something more generalizable and principled?

I think the problem is that it's really hard to find an actual 'principle' that doesn't have some problem.  For example, our old principle was "do not special case modules.  capture everything by value."  That was principled, but broke down badly with modules (forcing people to do things like 'require on the inside').  Similarly, we tried the principle of capturing all modules by by 'import'.  But this ends up breaking user-code itself because now we don't actually properly serialize code that can work properly since our serialization strategy does not properly mimic the user's actual code layout (and trying to do so would be a large undertaking).

This approach tries to accomplish the following:

1. allow users to write the code in idiomatic TS/JS, and have the semantics for all their module code behave as expected.
2. have a capturing story that has simple and explainable semantics.
3. allow people to author  a single project.json that defines both the deployment-time and cloud-runtime-time configuration, with reasonable outputs for both environments.

After this PR we have the above.  However, there is one specific issue a user could potentially trip themselves into.  Namely, they end up wanting to actually capture a pulumi module reference as a reference.  

Right now we can't think of any good reason why someone would want to do this.  And, instead of having it silently blow up, i thought it would be good to at least have us let the user know they're doing something bad.  

Note: In the future, if we think there is a good reason for this.  We can definitely relax this restriction.  But i'd rather start with a good working system, with a single non-onerous restriction, and only relax later if we really think it's necessary.

The argument is a string array in the API-reference. Arrays in the API should be represented by a `ListField`, which handles some details of how arrays works. Each value in the array is a string, so the type is `six.text_type`. Using `Field(list)` will work in many cases, but there are some edge cases where it breaks. You also lose the type-support, since a python list can contain values of any type, while the k8s API only accepts strings in this case.
